content:
← partihaus partihaus curates objects with a clear parti — selected for function, form, and longevity
timeless over trendy · quality over imitation · intentional design this collection is for those who think about design — and those who gift them
title: about

content:
← products kitchen
Barbry Salt & Pepper Mills polished stainless steel
Designed by Georg Jensen, the Barbry mills take the most overlooked object on any table and give it a reason to stay out. The tapered steel bodies taper toward a dark oak base — a detail that grounds each piece and gives the pair a quiet visual logic together.
The ceramic grinding mechanism is smooth and precise. The exterior requires no explanation — the form communicates function without labels, without color coding, without text.
selected because most salt and pepper mills are hidden for a reason — these are not. They belong on the table before anyone sits down.
designer
Georg Jensen
materials
stainless steel, oak
origin
Denmark
made for
dining table, permanent display
find it here →
title: Barbry Salt & Pepper Mills

content:
← products tabletop
Blomus Alpha Decanting Carafe minimal curved glass
Made by Blomus, the Alpha carafe is defined by a single curve — the glass swells at the base and narrows upward to a steel stopper. There are no straight lines. The form is arrived at purely through function: the wide base maximizes surface area for decanting, the narrow neck controls pour.
It works equally well for wine, water, or juice. Empty, it reads as a sculptural object. The steel stopper is the only hard element — a deliberate contrast that anchors the softness of the glass.
selected because it makes any table look more considered — and because it's the kind of object that prompts questions from guests.
designer
Blomus
materials
borosilicate glass, steel
origin
Germany
made for
dining table, wine, water
find it here →
title: Blomus Alpha Decanting Carafe

content:
← products kitchen
Emma French Press steel with beechwood handle
Designed by Stelton, the Emma french press applies the same restraint the Danish studio is known for in tableware to the morning ritual of coffee. The steel body is mirror-polished; the beechwood handle is warm to the touch and ages gracefully with use.
Most french presses are purely utilitarian. The Emma is that too — but it also earns a permanent spot on the counter. The proportions are considered enough that it functions as an object even when it isn't being used.
selected because good coffee deserves better equipment — and because this is the rare kitchen tool that requires no hiding.
designer
Stelton
materials
stainless steel, beechwood
origin
Denmark
made for
daily use, counter display
find it here →
title: Emma French Press

content:
← products kitchen
iittala Kartio Tumbler clear medium glass, set of 8
Designed by Kaj Franck in 1958, the Kartio tumbler has been in continuous production ever since — which is the clearest possible endorsement of a design. The geometry is simple: a gentle taper from base to rim, nothing more. No texture, no ornamentation, no visual noise.
Franck believed that everyday objects should be democratic and enduring. The Kartio is both. It stacks neatly, feels right in the hand, and suits every table from breakfast to dinner.
selected because a set of eight means you actually use them — and because these are the glasses you keep for twenty years.
designer
Kaj Franck, 1958
materials
soda-lime glass
origin
Finland
made for
everyday use, any table

title: iittala Kartio Tumbler

content:
← products lighting
Kaiser Idell Table Lamp bauhaus steel, high-gloss finish
Designed by Christian Dell in 1933 for Kaiser & Co., the Idell lamp is one of the most resolved objects to come out of the Bauhaus era. Its articulated arm, domed shade, and weighted base form a system with no unnecessary parts.
Decades of reissues have confirmed what the original proved: the proportions are simply correct. The high-gloss finish catches light in a way that makes the object itself as interesting as what it illuminates.
selected because it is one of the few lamps that works as well on a desk as it does as a sculptural object in a room.
designer
Christian Dell, 1933
materials
steel, high-gloss lacquer
origin
Germany
made for
desk, side table, display

title: Kaiser Idell Table Lamp
Kontra Fruit Bowl

content:
← products tabletop
Kubus Candleholder architectural steel design
Designed by Mogens Lassen in 1962, the Kubus is a study in structural honesty. Four candle spires rise from an open steel cube — the geometry does all the work, nothing is decorative that isn't also structural.
Lassen was an architect, and it shows. The Kubus reads differently depending on whether the candles are lit or not — two distinct objects sharing one form. Produced today by by Lassen in Denmark, faithful to the original drawings.
selected because it is equally at home on a dining table or a mantle — and it looks as good empty as it does in use.
designer
Mogens Lassen, 1962
materials
powder-coated steel
origin
Denmark
made for
dining table, mantle, display

title: Kubus Candleholder

content:
← products Pantone Mug color-matched ceramic
The Pantone mug is a piece of graphic design history made functional. Each mug is glazed in an exact Pantone color match and labeled with the corresponding chip number — the same system that designers, printers, and manufacturers worldwide use as their shared language of color.
It's a rare object that works as a gift regardless of whether the recipient is a designer or not. For designers, it's a knowing reference. For everyone else, it's simply a beautifully colored ceramic mug with an interesting number on it. The box alone earns it a place under a tree.
selected because it's the most giftable object in the collection — instantly understood, immediately personal, endlessly collectible.
designer
Pantone LLC
materials
glazed ceramic
origin
Various
made for
daily use, gifting, collecting
find it here →
title: Pantone Mug

title: products

content:
← products tabletop
Turning Tray dual-tone laminated wood
Originally designed by Finn Juhl in 1953 for the United Nations headquarters in New York, the Turning Tray was made to allow delegates to pass documents across a table without standing. The mechanism is elegant: two layers of laminated wood rotate independently on a central pin.
The two-tone colorway — one face lighter, one darker — means the tray looks different depending on which way it faces. It is among the most quietly clever objects in Danish design history, now produced by House of Finn Juhl.
selected because it has a story worth telling — and because functionality with this much elegance is genuinely rare.
designer
Finn Juhl, 1953
materials
laminated wood
origin
Denmark
